paper.title_en: Can cosmology resolve the neutron lifetime tension ?
paper.abstract_en: The neutron lifetime τn is a fundamental input to Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN) that controls the neutron-to-proton ratio at the onset of element production and thus the primordial helium mass fraction YP. A persistent  ≃ 4σ tension exists between beam-based experiments (τnbeam≈888.0±2.0 s) and cryogenic bottle/trap experiments (τnbottle≈879.4±0.6 s). The discrepancy Δτn ≈ 8.6 s translates into a predicted shift in the primordial helium mass fraction of ΔYP ≈ 0.0018, or roughly 0.7% of the Standard-Model value. We critically assess whether next-generation astrophysical observations of YP, achievable with integral-field-unit (IFU) spectroscopy of metal-poor Blue Compact Dwarf (BCD) galaxies combined with precision spectrophotometric calibration, can resolve this tension. Using a Fisher-matrix forecast over the parameter space {τn, Ωbh2, Neff}, we show that a 0.1% determination of YP, when combined with a sub-percent measurement of (D/H)P and Planck-quality CMB data, yields an astrophysical constraint on τn whose precision depends critically on the treatment of the effective number of neutrino species Neff. If Neff is fixed to its Standard Model value 3.044 — motivated by the absence of new light species — a 0.1% determination of YP combined with current D/H data already reaches σ(τn) ≃ 1.3 s, providing  > 6σ discrimination. If Neff is treated as a free parameter, Planck-quality CMB data are insufficient to break the Neff–τn degeneracy in YP, and a next-generation CMB experiment (CMB-S4) with σ(Neff) ≃ 0.03 is required, yielding σ(τn) ≃ 4.3 s at the  ∼ 2σ threshold. In all cases an independent measurement of the baryon density — from either primordial D/H or the CMB — is an essential prerequisite, since τn and Ωbh2 are completely degenerate in YP alone.
